raw_text: Frank Wittchow, "Vater und Onkel: Julius Caesar und das finale des Aeneis." Gymnasium 1 12 (2005) 45-69. Smolenaars senses the evolution of Venus from love-goddess to Venus Genetrix, and the dynastic mythology of Augustus, in the encounter of Venus and Vulcan in Aeneid 8.370ff. The light-hearted scene activates the double identity of Venus, love-goddess and mother. The humor is irreverent and Alexandrian. Noonan argues that the ending of the epic is "part of the discourse of late Republican politics" (p. 43), and that contemporary civic standards are implied in the climactic battles in Latium (p. 34).

raw_text: Philip Thibodeau, "Can Vergil Cry? Epicu 1.24," CJ 98 (2003) 243-256. Clark shows that Vergil's poetry pervades Ho and thematically in a manner designed to pioneers a new breakthrough-even as Hor poetical and political levels. Horace reworks V the aftermath of Prometheus and his allusion to the civil wars in Eclogue 4. Deremetz studies the Sibyl in Vergil, Ovid, and Silius Italicus. Foulon engages with the Vergilian Sibyl in Propertius and Tibullus. Farrell argues that the ille ego 'false preface' to the epic was echoed by Ovid in his own career statement, which moves from an earlier ludic phase to rueful retrospective from exile. Galinsky detects verbal affinities between Horace's Cleopatra and Vergil's Dido and argues for creative interchange. Horace's Cleopatra in Odes 1.37 is one of many models for tragic Dido. Gosling discovers intratextual echoes between Vergil and Ovid. Ovid uses verbal allusion, rhetorical colouring and metrical emphasis to highlight his characterisation of Mezentius, redirecting the story to highlight Mezentius' brutality. Paul Miller engages with Dido's letter to Aeneas in Ovid's Heroides suggesting that Dido's voice opens up a dialogue with the monologic tendency of the epic which destabilises its version of events. John Miller suggests that Vergil's presentation of Actian Apollo reverberates in Propertius 2.4 which reveals knowledge of Aeneid 8 and Aeneid 1. Propertius 4.6 in Miller's assessment stresses Callimachean connections of Apollo's Delian origin, and provides a different poetic slant on Apollo's manifestation.

raw_text: J. Trappes-Lomax, "Hiatus in Vergil and in Horace's Odes." PCPS 50 (2004) 141-158. Harrison notices that the word imago (= simile) in Vergil, Ovid and the Flavian poets is occasionally unearthed within, or in the immediate context of , an epic simile. The device is an aid to metaphorical effects: for intertextual cross-referencing to similes in previous epics, for intratextual purposes to link the relevant simile with previous material in the same epic, and for poetically ambiguous means to exploit the wide semantic range involved in the usage of imago. Alexander G. McKay 79

raw_text: Roger Rees, ed., Romane Memento: Vergil in the Fourth Century. London 2004. Rev: McKay, NECJ 32 (2005) 285-287. Dominik studies Statius' epilogue to the Thebaid 12.810-19 in a different light. Critics have generally read the lines as confession of poetic inferiority to Vergil; Dominik argues that the verses are meant to encourage readers to respect the intellectual merits of the epic and its role within a larger tradition. Feeney treats Statius' undernourished Achilleid from the standpoint of the sexual ambiguity of Achilles, the legacy from Ovidian and Vergilian epic, and the poet's concern to establish a distinctive creative difference between Thebaid and Achilleid. Quintus of Smyrna's 4th century capo lavoro soars in a scholarly revival. McGill provides the first systematic study of mythological and secular centos. Morzadec explores what 80 Annual Bibliography inspired Statius to plant imperial panegyric in the visionary speech (IV. 3.123-163).

raw_text: David Quint, "The Virgilian Coordinates of Paradise Lost." REPRESENTING 177-197. Joy Connolly deals with current work on the republican tradition i literature and political thought. She focuses on translations of Verg by the 17th century theorist James Harrington in a study of reception (114-126) of Vergil's pastorals and epic. David Quint assesses Milton's dark reworking of what appears to be positive politica material and reveals the negativity of the Vergilian original: the statesman simile in Book I "hints at the violence built into the settlement that Augustus imposed on the Roman political world" (p. 1 80), and the City of Pandemonium exposes the dark, hellish side of Vergil's Carthage and Rome. The fallen Satan and Adam resemble Aeneas after his shipwreck, starting afresh from the bottom.

raw_text: Mike Pincombe, "Gloomy Orion: Eliot, Marlowe, Virgil." N&Q 50 (2003) 329-330. Johnson reviews Lowell's "Falling Asleep over the Aeneid." to discover and assess Lowell's ambivalence towards the epic and military imperialism in general. His vehicle is the 'exquisite ambiguity' of the funeral of Pallas. The fascistic color, sexual imagery, and ties with Lowell's own family losses in the Civil War are duly explored. The high-tide of New Criticism and foreshadowings of "Harvard-School" Vergilian scholarship are intriguingly displayed by Ralph Johnson. Lowrie's essay provides an exercise in Vergilian reception through Maurice Blanchot's thoughts on Hermann Broch's The Death of Virgil which he embodies in a short story, "The Moment of My Death." (Blanchot's reversal lays emphasis neither on self-sacrifice nor on sparing the epic at Vergil's death; the man survives and the written work is lost.)

raw_text: Christopher Lehman, "Offend the Gods and Love Obey." Vergilius 50 (2004) 108-130. Colette traces Advent and Christmas sequences, and tropes and chanted breviary prayers, as testimony to the Sibyl as prophetess. The reliance of the works on a single monody suggests that they were very ancient. Fitzgerald (199-210) offers an analysis of Berlioz's Les Troyens and highlights female dominance in the action There is a large scale expansion of Cassandra's role in Part I as counterpart to Dido's premier role at Carthage. The composer chose to stress betrayal and destruction rather than triumph: the opera literally transforms the epic. Isabelle His traces the Sibyl in music that highlights Christ's birth, the Passion and the Resurrection. Twelve 16th century versions exhibit individual, iconographie model of the Sibyl. Maurice Ohana's three 20th century compositions include the Sibyl. Isabelle His studies the libretto, and accents reversals of the Aeneid in plot, performance, characterization, and political implications. McMASTER UNIVERSITY
